concepts.title: Working Rule adopted to solve Algebraic limits in cases when the independent variable tends to ∞ or -∞
concepts.conceptBody:
Method to Evaluate the Limit of a Function

There are a number of methods to evaluate the limit of a function but for the sake of convenience, we divide the problems in two types. If $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is a function of $\text{x}$ then $\text{x}$ is the independent variable.

Type I. We will call those problems in which the independent variable tends to $\infty $ or $-\infty $ as problems of Type I.

Type II. We will call those problems in which independent variable $\text{x}$ does not tend to $\infty $ or $-\infty $ as problems of Type II.

Examples : (i) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to \infty }}{{\left( 1+\frac{\text{a}}{\text{x}} \right)}^{\text{x}}}$ is a problem of type I, as here independent variable $\text{x}\to \infty $

(ii) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to 0}}\frac{{{\text{a}}^{\text{x}}}-\text{bx}}{\text{x}}$ is a problem of type II as here independent variable $\text{x}$ does not tend to $\infty $ or $-\infty $.

Note : Here $\alpha $ is the independent variable and $\text{x}$ is a constant.

Again for the sake of convenience we divide problems of each type in three categories. 

Category A. Problems involving only algebraic functions.

Category B. Problems involving non-zero constant powers of sin, cos, tan, cot, sec or cosec of a variable angle.

Category C. Problems involving exponential or logarithmic functions.

Examples: 

(i) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to \text{a}}}\frac{{{\text{x}}^{4}}-{{\text{a}}^{4}}}{\text{x}-\text{a}}$. This problem is of category A of type II.

(ii) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}\frac{x+\text{sin}x}{x}$. This problem is of category B of type II.

(iii) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 0}}{{(\text{sin}x)}^{\text{tan}x}}$. This problem is of category $C$ of type II.

(iv) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to 2}}\frac{\text{log}\left( \text{x}-1 \right)}{\text{x}-2}$. This problem is of category $\text{C}$ of type II.

(v) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to \infty }}\frac{{{x}^{2}}+{{x}^{2}}+1}{{{x}^{4}}+2}$. This problem is of category A of type I.

(vi) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to \infty }}2\text{nsin}\frac{1}{n}$. This problem is of category B of type I.

(vii) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to \infty }}{{\left( 1+\frac{1}{\text{x}} \right)}^{2\text{x}}}\cdot $ This problem is of category $\text{C}$ of type $\text{I}$.

concepts.title: Solved Problems on Algebraic Limits when the independent variable tends to ∞ or -∞
concepts.conceptBody:
Problems involving algebraic expressions

Type I. Category (A) 

Working Rule: 

1. First of all simplify the given expression in the form of $\frac{N}{D}$.

2. (i) Then divide each terms of the numerator and denominator by ${{x}^{n}}$ where $x$ is the independent variable and $\text{n}$ is the highest power of $\text{x}$ in the numerator and denominator taken together.

(ii) Then put $\frac{\text{c}}{{{\text{x}}^{\text{k}}}}=0$, where $\text{c}$ is a constant and $\text{k}>0$. This is because $\frac{\text{c}}{{{\text{x}}^{\text{k}}}}\to 0$ as $\text{x}\to \infty$.

3. Alternatively, take out term containing highest power of $\text{x}$ in the numerator and denominator as common and finally put

${{\text{x}}^{\text{k}}} =0,\text{ if }\text{ k}<0$

$=\infty ,\text{ if }\text{ k}>0$

$=1,\text{ if }\text{ k}=0$

Tip: Simply check the powers in numerator & denominator.

for $\frac{a{{x}^{n}}+b{{x}^{n}}+\ldots }{p{{x}^{d}}+q{{x}^{d-1}}+\ldots }$ if

(i) $\text{n}>\text{d}$; result is $\infty $

(ii) $\text{n}<\text{d}$; result is 0

(iii) $\text{n}=\text{d}$; limit is $\left( \frac{\text{a}}{\text{p}} \right)$

 

(a) Find \(\underset{x\to \infty }{\mathop{Lt}}\,\frac{{{x}^{4}}+2{{x}^{3}}+3}{2{{x}^{4}}-x+2}\)

(b) Find $\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{Lt}}\,\left( \frac{{{1}^{2}}}{{{n}^{3}}}+\frac{{{2}^{2}}}{{{n}^{3}}}+\frac{{{3}^{2}}}{{{n}^{3}}}+\ldots +\frac{{{n}^{2}}}{{{n}^{3}}} \right)$ 

Solution: 

(a) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to \infty }}\frac{{{x}^{4}}+2{{x}^{3}}+3}{2{{x}^{4}}-x+2}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to \infty }}\frac{1+\frac{2}{x}+\frac{3}{{{x}^{4}}}}{-\frac{1}{{{x}^{3}}}+\frac{2}{{{x}^{4}}}}=\frac{1}{2}$

$\left[ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Since }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\to \infty ,\frac{2}{\text{x}}\to 0,\frac{3}{{{\text{x}}^{4}}}\to 0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{2}{{{\text{x}}^{4}}}\to 0 \right]$

(b) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{n}\to \infty }}\left( \frac{{{1}^{2}}}{{{\text{n}}^{3}}}+\frac{{{2}^{2}}}{{{\text{n}}^{3}}}+\frac{{{3}^{2}}}{{{\text{n}}^{3}}}+\ldots +\frac{{{\text{n}}^{2}}}{{{\text{n}}^{3}}} \right)$

$~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{n\to \infty }}\frac{{{1}^{2}}+{{2}^{2}}+{{3}^{2}}+\ldots +{{n}^{2}}}{{{n}^{3}}}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{n\to \infty }}\frac{n\left( n+1 \right)\left( 2n+1 \right)}{6{{n}^{3}}}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{n\to \infty }}\frac{\left( n+1 \right)\left( 2n+1 \right)}{6{{n}^{2}}}$

$~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{n\to \infty }}\frac{n\left( 2{{n}^{2}}+3n+1 \right)}{6{{n}^{2}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{n\to \infty }}\frac{2+\frac{3}{n}+\frac{1}{{{n}^{2}}}}{6}=\frac{2}{6}=\frac{1}{3}$

 

(a) Find $\underset{x\to \infty }{\mathop{Lt}}\,\sqrt{x}\left( \sqrt{x+c}-\sqrt{x} \right)$ 
(b) $L{{t}_{x\to -\infty }}\left( \sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+4x}-\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-4x} \right)$ 

(c) I : $li{{m}_{x\to \infty }}\frac{\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+1}-\sqrt[3]{{{x}^{2}}+1}}{\sqrt[4]{{{x}^{4}}+1}-\sqrt[5]{{{x}^{4}}+1}}$ 

Solution: 

(a) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to \infty }}\sqrt{\text{x}}\left( \sqrt{\text{x}+\text{c}}-\sqrt{\text{x}} \right)$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to \infty }}\frac{\sqrt{x}\left( \sqrt{x+c}-\sqrt{x} \right)\left( \sqrt{x+c}+\sqrt{x} \right)}{\left( \sqrt{x+c}+\sqrt{x} \right)}$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to \infty }}\frac{\sqrt{x}\left( x+c-x \right)}{\left( \sqrt{x+c}+\sqrt{x} \right)}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to \infty }}\frac{c\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x+c}+\sqrt{x}}$

$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to \infty }}\frac{c}{\frac{\sqrt{x+c}}{\sqrt{x}}+\frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}}}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to \infty }}\frac{c}{\sqrt{\frac{x+c}{x}}+1}$

$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to \infty }}\frac{c}{\sqrt{1+\frac{c}{x}}+1}=\frac{c}{1+1}=\frac{c}{2}$

(b) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to -\infty }}\left( \sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+4\text{x}}-\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}-4\text{x}} \right)$

=$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to -\infty }}\frac{\left( \sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+4x}-\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-4x} \right)\left( \sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+4x}+\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-4x} \right)}{\left( \sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+4x}+\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-4x} \right)}$

=$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to -\infty }}\frac{\left( {{x}^{2}}+4x \right)-\left( {{x}^{2}}-4x \right)}{\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+4x}+\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-4x}}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to -\infty }}\frac{8x}{\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+4x}+\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-4x}}$

=$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to -\infty }}\frac{8}{\frac{\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+4x}+\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-4x}}{x}}$

=$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to -\infty }}\frac{8}{\sqrt{\frac{{{x}^{2}}+4x}{{{x}^{2}}}}+\sqrt{\frac{{{x}^{2}}-4x}{{{x}^{2}}}}}$

$\left[ {} \right.$ Here $\text{x}<0\because \text{x}=-\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}$ for example $\left. -4=-\sqrt{{{(-4)}^{2}}}=-\sqrt{16} \right]$

$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to -\infty }}\frac{8}{\sqrt{1+\frac{4}{x}}+\sqrt{1-\frac{4}{x}}}=\frac{8}{1+1}=\frac{8}{2}=4$

(c) dividing the numerator & denominator by $\text{x}$ (which is the greatest power of $\text{x}$ possible)

$\begin{matrix} \underset{x\to \infty }{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{\frac{\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+1}}{x}-\frac{\sqrt[3]{{{x}^{2}}+1}}{x}}{\frac{\sqrt[4]{{{x}^{4}}+1}}{x}-\frac{\sqrt[5]{{{x}^{4}}+1}}{x}} \\ =\underset{x\to \infty }{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{\frac{\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+1}}{x}-\sqrt[3]{\frac{{{x}^{2}}+1}{x}}}{\frac{\sqrt[4]{{{x}^{4}}+1}}{x}-\frac{\sqrt[5]{{{x}^{4}}+1}}{x}} \\ \end{matrix}$

=$\underset{x\to \infty }{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{\frac{{{\left( {{x}^{2}}+1 \right)}^{1/2}}}{x}-\frac{{{\left( {{x}^{2}}+1 \right)}^{1/3}}}{x}}{\frac{{{\left( {{x}^{4}}+1 \right)}^{1/4}}}{x}-\frac{{{\left( {{x}^{4}}+1 \right)}^{1/5}}}{x}}$

=$\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\left(\frac{x{2}+1}{x{2}}\right)^{1 / 2}-\left(\frac{x{2}+1}{x{3}}\right)^{1 / 3}}{\left(\frac{\left(x{4}+1\right)}{x{4}}\right)^{1 / 4}-\left(\frac{x{4}+1}{x{5}}\right)^{1 / 5}}=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\left(1+\frac{1}{x{2}}\right){1 / 2}-\left(\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{x{3}}\right){1 / 3}}{\left(1+\frac{1}{x{4}}\right){1 / 4}-\left(\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{x{5}}\right){1 / 5}}$

as $x\to \infty \frac{1}{{{x}^{p}}}\to 0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }($ for $p>1)$

$=\frac{\left( 1+0 \right)-0}{\left( 1+0 \right)-0}=1$

concepts.title: Methods implemented to solve Algebraic limits when the independent variable tends to a fixed real number
concepts.conceptBody:
Problems in which algebraic functions occur

Type II. Category A. 

When $\text{x}\to \text{a}$, where $\text{a}$ is a fixed real number. 

Working Rule: 

Limits of functions involving only algebraic functions and when independent variable does not tend to $\infty $ or $-\infty $ can be evaluated by using the following formula

$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to a}}\frac{{{x}^{n}}-{{a}^{n}}}{x-a}=n{{a}^{n-1}}$

There are other methods also to evaluate such limits.

There are 3 methods to solve these kind of questions.

(a) Direct substitution 

for a limit, $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to a}}f\left( x \right)$, we can directly substitute $x=a$ in the limit only if the following constraints are not there

  • $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to a}}f\left( x \right)$ is of type 1 (intermediate form)
  • $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{\text{x}\to \text{a}}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is undefined.

(b) Factorization 

Factorization method can also be used to solve these kind of questions.

for $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to a}}\frac{P\left( x \right)}{Q\left( x \right)}$, if $P\left( a \right)=0\And \text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to a}}Q\left( a \right)=0$

(note $Q\left( a \right)\ne 0$, otherwise the function is undefined) then we can say that $\left( x-a \right)$ is a factor of both $\text{P}\left( \text{x} \right)\And \text{Q}\left( \text{x} \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to a}}\frac{P\left( x \right)}{Q\left( x \right)}=\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to a}}\frac{\left( x-a \right)N\left( x \right)}{\left( x-a \right)D\left( x \right)}=\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to a}}\frac{N\left( x \right)}{D\left( x \right)}$

repeat this procedure of cancellation until you get to a useful result.

(c) Rationalization 

If we get $\frac{0}{0}$ form in the problems involving roots then we must rationalize them to get the common factor, which will be cancelled out.

concepts.title: Solved problems based on factorization when the independent variable tends to a fixed real number
concepts.conceptBody:
Illustrations based on factorization 

(a) $li{{m}_{x\to a}}\left( \frac{{{x}^{3}}-{{a}^{3}}}{{{x}^{2}}-ax} \right)$ 
(b) $li{{m}_{x\to 3}}\frac{{{x}^{3}}-{{x}^{2}}-3x-9}{{{x}^{2}}-4x+3}$ 
(c) $li{{m}_{x\to 4}}\frac{{{x}^{3}}-2{{x}^{2}}-9x+4}{{{x}^{2}}-2x-8}$ 
(d) $li{{m}_{x\to 1}}\frac{{{x}^{7}}-2{{x}^{5}}+1}{{{x}^{3}}-3{{x}^{2}}+2}$ 

Solution: 

(a) Given $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to a}}\left( \frac{{{x}^{3}}-{{a}^{3}}}{{{x}^{2}}-ax} \right)$

we know ${{x}^{3}}-{{a}^{3}}=\left( x-a \right)\left( {{x}^{2}}+ax+{{a}^{2}} \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to a}}\frac{\left( x-a \right)\left( {{x}^{2}}+ax+{{a}^{2}} \right)}{x\left( x-a \right)}$

$=\underset{x\to a}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{\left( {{x}^{2}}+ax+{{a}^{2}} \right)}{x}=\frac{3{{a}^{2}}}{a}=3a$

(b) Given $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to 3}}\frac{{{x}^{3}}-{{x}^{2}}-3x-9}{{{x}^{2}}-4x+3}$

if we put $\text{x}=3$ in numerator $\And $ denominator we get 0 in both, i.e. $\left( \text{x}-3 \right)$ is a factor of both numerator & denominator.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ limit becomes, $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{\text{x}\to 3}}\frac{\left( \text{x}-3 \right)\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+2\text{x}+3 \right)}{\left( \text{x}-3 \right)\left( \text{x}-1 \right)}$

now we can put $\text{x}=3$

$=\frac{9+6+3}{2}=\frac{18}{2}=9$

(c) When $\text{x}=4$ numerator and denominator become zero]

$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 4}}\frac{{{x}^{3}}-2{{x}^{2}}-9x+4}{{{x}^{2}}-2x-8}$

$~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 4}}\frac{{{x}^{3}}-4{{x}^{2}}-2{{x}^{2}}-8x-x+4}{{{x}^{2}}-4x+2x-8}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 4}}\frac{\left( x-4 \right)\left( {{x}^{2}}+2x-1 \right)}{\left( x-4 \right)\left( x+2 \right)}$

$~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 4}}\frac{{{x}^{2}}-2x-1}{x+2}=\frac{23}{6}$

Second Method : $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 4}}\frac{{{x}^{3}}-2{{x}^{2}}-9x+4}{{{x}^{2}}-2x-8}$

=$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 4}}\frac{\left( \frac{{{x}^{3}}-{{4}^{3}}}{x-4} \right)\left( x-4 \right)-2\left( \frac{{{x}^{2}}-{{4}^{2}}}{x-4} \right)\left( x-4 \right)\left( \frac{x-4}{x-4} \right)\left( x-4 \right)}{\left( \frac{{{x}^{2}}-{{4}^{2}}}{x-4} \right)\left( x-4 \right)-2\left( \frac{x-4}{x-4} \right)\left( x-4 \right)}$

=$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 4}}\frac{\frac{{{x}^{3}}-{{4}^{3}}}{x-4}-2\left( \frac{{{x}^{2}}-{{4}^{2}}}{x-4} \right)-9\left( \frac{x-4}{x-4} \right)}{\frac{{{x}^{2}}-{{4}^{2}}}{x-4}-2\left( \frac{x-4}{x-4} \right)}$

$=\frac{{{3.4}^{2}}-{{2.2.4}^{1}}-9}{{{2.4}^{1}}-2}=\frac{23}{6}$

(d) When $\text{x}=1$ numerator and denominator both become zero and hence $\left( \text{x}-1 \right)$ is a factor of both

Now $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 1}}\frac{{{x}^{7}}-2{{x}^{5}}+1}{{{x}^{3}}-3{{x}^{2}}+2}$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 1}}\frac{{{x}^{7}}-{{x}^{6}}+{{x}^{6}}-{{x}^{5}}-{{x}^{5}}+{{x}^{4}}-{{x}^{4}}+{{x}^{3}}-{{x}^{3}}+{{x}^{2}}-{{x}^{2}}+x-x+1}{{{x}^{3}}-{{x}^{2}}-2{{x}^{2}}+2x+2}$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 1}}\frac{{{x}^{6}}\left( x-1 \right)+{{x}^{5}}\left( x-1 \right)-{{x}^{4}}\left( x-1 \right)-{{x}^{3}}\left( x-1 \right)-{{x}^{2}}\left( x-1 \right)-x\left( x-1 \right)-\left( x-1 \right)}{{{x}^{2}}\left( x-1 \right)-2x\left( x-1 \right)-2\left( x-1 \right)}$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 1}}\frac{\left( {{x}^{6}}+{{x}^{5}}-{{x}^{4}}-{{x}^{3}}-{{x}^{2}}-x-1 \right)}{{{x}^{2}}-2x-2}=\frac{-3}{-3}=1$

Second Method : $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 1}}\frac{{{x}^{7}}-2{{x}^{5}}+1}{{{x}^{3}}-3{{x}^{2}}+2}$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 1}}\frac{\left( \frac{{{x}^{7}}-{{1}^{7}}}{x-1} \right)\left( x-1 \right)-2\left( \frac{{{x}^{5}}-{{1}^{5}}}{x-1} \right)\left( x-1 \right)}{\left( \frac{{{x}^{3}}-{{1}^{3}}}{x-1} \right)\left( x-1 \right)-3\left( \frac{{{x}^{2}}-{{1}^{2}}}{x-1} \right)\left( x-1 \right)}$

$~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 1}}\frac{7{{(1)}^{6}}-2\left( 5{{(1)}^{4}} \right)}{3{{(1)}^{2}}-3\left( 2\left( 1 \right) \right)}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 1}}\frac{7-10}{3-6}$

$~=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 1}}\frac{-3}{-3}=1$

concepts.title: Solved problems using formula when the independent variable tends to a fixed real number
Solved problems based on rationalization when the independent variable tends to a fixed real number
concepts.conceptBody:
Illustrations based on rationalization 

(a) $li{{m}_{x\to 0}}\frac{\sqrt{1+{{x}^{2}}}-\sqrt{1+x}}{x}$ 
(b) $li{{m}_{x\to 2}}\frac{x-2}{\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-4}+\sqrt{x-2}}$ 
(c) $L{{t}_{x\to a}}\frac{\sqrt{a+2x}-\sqrt{3x}}{\sqrt{3a+x}-2\sqrt{x}},a\ne 0$ 

Solution: 

(a) [Given function $\sqrt{1+{{x}^{2}}}-\sqrt{1+x}$ is of the form $\sqrt{a}-\sqrt{b}$]

(b) $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{\text{x}\to 2}}\frac{\text{x}-2}{\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}-4}+\sqrt{\text{x}-2}}\left[ \frac{0}{0} \right.$ form]

Second Method

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 2}}\frac{x-2}{\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-4}+\sqrt{x-2}}=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 2}}\frac{x-2}{\sqrt{\left( x-2 \right)\left( x+2 \right)}+\sqrt{x}-2}$

$=\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 2}}\frac{x-2}{\sqrt{\left( x-2 \right)}+\sqrt{x+2+1}}\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{x\to 2}}\frac{\sqrt{x-2}}{\sqrt{x+2}+1}=\frac{0}{2+1}=0[I.I.T.78]$

(c) Required limit

examples.title: If $\lim _{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{x^n-2^n}{x-2}=80$, where $n$ is a positive integer, then $n=$
The value of $\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{x^2+b x+4}{x^2+a x+5}\right)$ is
